title: Debts placed on hold before 1 January 2017
description: Proposed law change to give us discretion not to offset some debts on hold.
keywords: Debt, paying, amount owed, how much you owe, owe, owe ATO, owing money, tax payments, payments, when to pay, debt owed, why I owe, owe tax, debt on hold, debts on hold, non-pursued debt, non-pursued debts, invisible debt, offsetting, offsetting of refund, offsetting of credits, why is my refund less than expected, re-raise, non-pursuit, aged debt, aged debts, non pursued debt, non pursued debts, non pursuit, tax refund is less, ato refund less than expected, existing debt on hold included in account balance, cost effective to collect, partial re-raise of non-pursuit, re-raise of non-pursuit

content_blocks.text: The law requires us to offset debts on hold using any refunds or credits you're entitled to.
Proposed changes to this law were announced in the 2024–25 Budget, giving us discretion not to offset in certain circumstances.
Proposed changes are expected to affect debts placed on hold before 1 January 2017 for:
The measure is not yet law.
You can check when your debt was placed on hold .
Included – debts on hold in the proposed measure

If the measure is passed, we can choose not to offset refunds or credits against debts that were placed on hold before 1 January 2017 and remain on hold. This would only apply to individuals, small businesses, and not-for-profit entities.
We are not currently including these debts on hold in account balances, and are not offsetting any refunds or credits you’re entitled to against these debts.
We remit the general interest charge that is applied to debts on hold when they are not included in account balances.
Not included – debts on hold not in the proposed measure
The proposed new law will not apply to:

items: debts that were placed on hold on or after 1 January 2017

debts that were placed on hold prior to 1 January 2017 for large businesses superannuation funds multinational groups taxpayers who, at the time, were viewed as part of a privately owned and wealthy group .

content_blocks.text: The law requires us to offset these debts using any refunds or credits to which taxpayers become entitled.
We're making it easier for you to keep track of these debts, and are now including these debts placed on hold in account balances .

content_text:
## Proposed law change
The law requires us to offset debts on hold using any refunds or credits you're entitled to.
Proposed changes to this law were announced in the 2024–25 Budget, giving us discretion not to offset in certain circumstances.
Proposed changes are expected to affect debts placed on hold before 1 January 2017 for:
- individuals
- small businesses
- not-for-profit entities.
The measure is not yet law.
You can check when your debt was placed on hold .
## Included – debts on hold in the proposed measure
If the measure is passed, we can choose not to offset refunds or credits against debts that were placed on hold before 1 January 2017 and remain on hold. This would only apply to individuals, small businesses, and not-for-profit entities.
We are not currently including these debts on hold in account balances, and are not offsetting any refunds or credits you’re entitled to against these debts.
We remit the general interest charge that is applied to debts on hold when they are not included in account balances.
## Not included – debts on hold not in the proposed measure
The proposed new law will not apply to:
- debts that were placed on hold on or after 1 January 2017
- debts that were placed on hold prior to 1 January 2017 for large businesses superannuation funds multinational groups taxpayers who, at the time, were viewed as part of a privately owned and wealthy group .
The law requires us to offset these debts using any refunds or credits to which taxpayers become entitled.
We're making it easier for you to keep track of these debts, and are now including these debts placed on hold in account balances .
